speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: You--

speaker: interviewer
text: Shouldn't limit yourself to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. You can-- if it's--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's important to have what-- you know, get everything you missed as far as the scene.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, that's only half of it. But I told--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know. Because like, we missed two good stories in Germany. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I felt like, oh no. I wanted to get when you had run through the deportations. We missed that. Oh, in France?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, see, I cut it short a little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's rolling. And anytime.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were telling me about when you left Hongkew. What was the date?

speaker: interviewee
text: March 20, 1947. And we walked as a ship. I don't know that it was that close. But anyway, I don't remember taking a rickshaw or anything. We probably have because we had one big box. And we thought we can't leave because we had no clothes to wear, to go anywhere. Oh my, I brought you my shoes, my Shanghai shoes, if you want to show them. I couldn't go on no ship to America with those shoes. So we got clothes from-- I don't know now, who the sponsor was-- Joint, I think. And they gave us clothes, whatever we needed, to look decent to go to America, shoes and dresses and clothes for the boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: What had you been wearing throughout your eight years in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Shanghai?

speaker: interviewee
text: --these shoes here. My everyday shoes and clogs, wooden clogs. And children went barefoot to school, often.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were your clothes like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe we had a skirt and a blouse. We washed it out at night and wore it again in the morning. And my husband-- we had no iron. My husband slept on his pants. He creased them like they should be. He put it under his sheet. And he slept on it. In the morning, it looks like it was ironed. We didn't have any luxuries like that. But we had enough clothes, then, to go on the ship.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there was a long line of people speaking English behind me or in front of me. And my husband said, you're the smart one. What do they say?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I said, no idea. Because they were Americans. And the little bit I had learned was English. And I hadn't heard it in so many years either.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't understand the word. But we got on. And was a troop carrier. So the men had to go to one side, and the women had to go to another side.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the little boy didn't know. And he was nine years old. He said, I go with my father. OK. And pretty soon, he got-- I think the ship hadn't even left. He got seasick. And he came to me. And I had only one bunk. It was five of them, up and down. And well, I gave up my bunk. And I slept sitting up till they found another bunk for me. But I had a ball. I didn't get sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I could eat all the goodies in the dining room. Butter, bread, stuff I hadn't seen in years. You know? Cheeses, all that good food.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the sick people, they said, don't tell us what you ate. We can't hear it. I said, well, I eat. I don't know what's the matter with you. I didn't get sick. And everybody around me got sick and couldn't even-- I said, oh, I had a good lunch. Well, don't talk about it. Please. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't get sick. I was lucky. How long did the journey--

speaker: interviewee
text: It was another three weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: It-- it took three weeks--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --from Shanghai to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we got out in Hawaii. And we took the streetcar to the beach, to Waikiki. The little kids swam in the water with their underwear.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we just stuck our feet in there. And everything was just unbelievable. The only thing was that, always in the back of your, mind why didn't my parents survive? Why didn't my husband's brother survive? You know what I mean? You could enjoy. But even like, life today, it never leaves my mind. I'm the lucky one. Why-- why couldn't the others enjoy-- plus the others-- all the people, you know, like we do? And made us a new life here, in America. We'll be here 50 years, next year. In-- when did we arrive? In March or something, also. I forgot now. So-- no, 7th of April, right. 7th of April. So this is always in the back of your mind. No matter what you enjoy, even nowadays. I don't cry every day, or I don't talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it stays with me. And it will stay with me. You know, you can-- when you lose parents the way parents go, by sickness, it's one thing. But being murdered and going that way, that you can't live down. You never can. Some people tell me, you have to lay it down now.

speaker: interviewer
text: No. Why should I? I can't. You wouldn't either. I don't depress anybody else with it. And I'm not depressed. But I can't enjoy like a person should.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I still count my blessings. But it just hangs over you. Not only the parents, but the brother-in-law, plus all the other relatives and six million people, no matter who they are.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you learn what happened to your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we only heard that one time, on the radio, when the-- in Shanghai, when the European war was over, that six million people got killed. And then when the war was over in Shanghai, we-- it was in the papers. We had our own paper in German. And then we could go to the consulate, Swiss Consulate gave you information. The Red Cross gave you information. We just inquired all over. And everybody was hoping that their relative or their parent or their child had survived. You didn't know for a long time. Then I thought maybe they're in Switzerland. And I thought, well, they know we were-- where-- where we were. You would think they have contacted you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still thought maybe they're sick, they can't contact you. You know what I mean? You didn't give up hope for a long time. So that you will never live it down. Nobody does. But that's why I'm saying we should look out and see that those things never, never happen to anybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't care who it is. We're all human beings. And we feel the same. We-- we eat and drink. We function the same. I mean, nobody has a right to do that to anybody. But it's very sad, the way it looks right now. Like in Bosnia, or even in Israel. It-- it just doesn't seem to be in the cards that that's possible, after all these things have happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's go back to when you landed in Waikiki. Where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we went only for one day, we stayed in-- in Honolulu, in Hawaii. And then we went on. And we stopped under the Golden Gate Bridge, in the middle of the night. And I went-- most people went up there and celebrated that. And then the Customs came right on the ship there, under the bridge. And we stopped. And then we went onto land. And they were-- with letters, H's go here. I's go there. And so then they told us where we're going to spend the night. And they sent us to a hotel there, in San Francisco, where the cable car turns, Powell Hotel. My son goes, still, in there every time he goes to San Francisco, looking if it's still there. And his daughter went in there now, and said, that's where my father spent the first night in the United States. So of course, for us, everything was great from then on. We get meals paid.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they called us in. And they said, what are your plans? And we said, we don't have any. Because we don't have any money. Who called you in?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- again, the Jewish organization. I think it was the Joint. And or HICEM, H-I-C-E-M. And they said, well, if you depend on us, you have to go stay out in the West here. We don't give you money to go back East. Because I was still wanting to go. I have my relatives there, uncles, aunts, cousins. And I-- they gave us a map. Showed us on a map where-- where's Portland, Oregon, they said.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, where is that? No idea. What did I know? New York, Chicago, Philadelphia

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So they showed us where Portland, Oregon is, on the West Coast. And my husband kept saying, we don't care. Wherever you send us, we're going. So then they gave us a choice between Portland, Oregon, Seattle, or Arizona. Then I inquired about the climate, and what work there is to be done.

speaker: interviewee
text: They said in Oregon and Washington it's the lumber industry. And in Arizona, it is getting very hot in the summertime. And this was out. We had enough from Shanghai, where we got used to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: But like I said, we slept outside and changed clothes, underwear, three times a day, especially. I didn't. But some people, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so we decided on Portland. Because there was an apartment available. If we would have gone to Seattle, we would stayed in a hotel till there's an apartment. After the war, they had a shortage of apartments here. Especially in the East, more than here. But also here. So we said, no, we want to go where we have our own apartment.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were some of the challenges you faced, adjusting to life in America?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first the language was a big barrier. And we bought the newspaper right away, in San Francisco. And I said to my husband, what'd you buy the paper for? He says, oh, we have to start learning somewhere. I'm not going to go no-- no lessons, like you probably will. I learn right on the spot. I read the paper. And so anyway, we came up to Portland on a train. And got in that little apartment. And then he looked through the papers and it said, furrier. Now, that he knew. I bought a dictionary, naturally. And he said, oh, I can do furrier for business. So he went into a department store there. And they said-- anyway, he couldn't fill that job. And he couldn't speak a word of English. So he didn't know what they told him. Then it was in there, busboy. He said, I can drive a bus. I said, well, you've got to have a license. I go. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went, found out its cleaning tables. He said, it's fine too. I don't care. I'll do that too.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then finally, so he was sick. He had to have surgery. And he got that free, in Portland. And one of the hospitals there had one room for those occasions. Not for only, immigrants, poor, other people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And after that we went to the-- what was it? Jewish Community Center or something like that. We were interviewed. And some men called while we were there and wanted a newcomer with business experience.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said me, me, me. So anyway, he got on in that store. And other Russian immigrant, he was older fellow. He spoke Yiddish with him. And he put him on the floor. He said, sell. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, that's how I learned business. So-- Did you work in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, naturally. So after Henry was in school, this committee sent me to [INAUDIBLE]. I don't no. I don't think you guys know that anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: They made sport clothes, ski clothes, and all that. I have no idea. I don't think they're in business anymore. And this man said to me, Mr. Hersh came to me and said, what do you want to do? I said, what I really want to do is go to school. And he said well, first you have to work, don't you? I said, well, I'd love to work. But I can't speak English, and I see what difficulties we have.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't know, must have been different than today. Because today the people who go to school, here are the Russian people in Tacoma. And they go to school. Anyway, he said, OK, come Monday morning, six o'clock. I never saw him again. They put me on a power machine, sewing. And I hated it with a passion. I don't even want to sew on a button.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told me that people told me how to handle the machine. I didn't understand it. Thread a needle, you know, how to do this. And so in-- in the lunchtime, they went in the bathroom and cried. And there was another lady who spoke German. She says, what's the matter with you? I said, I don't understand. How can I sew, which I don't like to begin with, and not very talented either.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I don't understand. She says, well, I can't tell you. Because I work on piecework. So many sleeves I sew, so many pant legs I sew, I get paid for.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what else would you like to do? I said, well, I'm a housewife. I can learn how to make coffee. Not the big machines, maybe, or make sandwiches. So after a week or two, she sent me to an Austrian immigrant, who had a little lunch counter in Portland. And he said, if you promise me not to speak German, I know you can't speak English. Just keep your mouth shut.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we have the business people coming for lunch here Downtown Tacoma-- or rather, Portland. it was next to the apartments, there by Anne Frank. It's still there. And I said no, I want to learn, that's all. And he said, OK. You watch this other girl. Because she's often drunk and doesn't show up in the morning. And you have to take over. And I was overwhelmed. I mean, pancakes, sundaes, milkshakes, no idea.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why is it that they did not want you to speak German, at the lunch counter?

speaker: interviewee
text: In his business, he didn't want me to speak German. He was afraid my friends would come in, have a cup of coffee or something. He said, he doesn't want to make a foreign place out of it. It's an American place. He has all-American clientele. And he didn't like that. And I understand that today. I didn't mind it then either. I understood it, as a business.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you come to Tacoma?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, then after we-- we-- he worked-- my husband worked for this Mr. Director, in Portland a while. And then he got on his own. He went and saw it [INAUDIBLE] you know, at this time it was-- he bought old batteries. Junk, junk, that was it. He bought the truck for $60. He was very enterprising. And he went on his own and earned money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then-- and I always had a job. I always-- when this was, I worked for him quite a while. And I was experienced, and I went somewhere else. So he said-- this new director said he has a store in Centralia, Washington. There's an opening in the Main Street. And he wants him to go to Centralia, run that store, and half profit goes to him. And half the profit goes to his son-in-law he has a no good son-in-law. So he doesn't earn any money. So he said, OK, what do you pay me for running the store? He said, nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: You get half of it. And I furnish the merchandise. So he said, well, we'll try. So I hated to go, but we went to Centralia. And we stayed there five years. And I also had to work, out, again. Because he didn't make enough. So then he had enough after five years. And we decided to go where his parents were aging. His parents lived with us in Portland for about a year. Then decided to go to San Francisco. And we had to go down there all the time. Mostly me, help-- help them. So he said, it's best we go there. So we're near the parents to help them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was fine with me. I loved it. And he said, well, he has merchandise in Centralia. He wound up the business with that Mr. Director.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he took it up to Tacoma. And here was a man who said, take it up to Bellingham. You can sell this stuff, a whole truckful. He got $900 in his pocket when he came back, and back to this man in Tacoma. And this one said there is a little store on the market, Market and 11th. It was a vegetable market at that time, and meat markets. And it's wonderful trade. And there's a little business. My brother handles it, and he always needs cash.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he went in. He said, how much cash you have? He says, $900. He said, you can't buy that-- that stuff. I-- I walk right out.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- he knew. He-- he saw what there was. So I came up. He said-- I said, you're crazy. He said, I'll make $2,000 out of the hand $900. And then we go to San Francisco. So he made $3,000 out of the $900. And he didn't want to leave Tacoma. He rented another place on Market Street, with a five-year lease or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was that. So I said, Tacoma? I want to go to San Francisco. Well, we have to go where the money is. I'm on my own, finally.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we have to send the boy to college. And this comes first. I know we will be able to. So he said, look for an apartment. And I'm not going in another apartment. The boy never had a room of his own. He was 17 years old then. He'd gone to a million schools.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, we're going to buy a house. With what? I said, I have $400 saved to go to New York. If you find one for $400 down payment, you can buy one. So I came up. And he already had looked, took me to this house, here. And I couldn't care less. I liked it. Two bedrooms, had no basement.

speaker: interviewee
text: People said, oh no. I said, it's a house. Let me move in. Later on we'll see what happens. So I'm still here, after 41 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have any grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I have three grandchildren. And we went to law school in Seattle. There was no law school in Tacoma.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who went to law school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Henry, my son. We had sent him to New York for the summer, to work. And one man there said, you can stay right here and work yourself up in our firm. And he said no, I go to school in Tacoma-- and UPS-- and I want to stay in the West. I don't like that New York. To visit, it's fine. But I don't want too stay here, live here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So anyway, he went to-- this man said, you might as well be a lawyer. You have a good head on his shoulders. He's on your shoulders. So he came home. He said, this man in Seattle said-- in New York said I could be a lawyer. What do you think? I said, be my guest.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] I never thought about that. He wanted to be an accountant. He really didn't know. He was a business man then too.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he is a lawyer. He went, three years, to Seattle. And he came back here, got married. And works now, for 30 years or longer, maybe, as a lawyer. And what are your grandchildren's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, David is 32. He's the oldest. And Susie is 30. And the youngest is Kim. She's 25. And she just got married in August, it was, a year.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her last name now is Isaac.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, is there anything I left out that you would like to add in this interview?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, maybe that was kind of a-- it wasn't so funny, but a remarkable story. When I was at that one year in school, to be a kindergarten teacher, and that year the teachers, they had an ounce of Jewish blood somewhere in the back maybe great grandparents. They were-- they had to go. And they replaced the teachers with SA and SS people, these storm troopers were the worst ones. They wore the black uniforms. And they introduced a new subject, race theory. And they were telling that-- the-- the Aryans, only Aryans have a right to live in Germany. And Aryans. No Jews. And Aryans are the all-German background.

speaker: interviewer
text: And there's blond hair and blue eyes. And not only that, but a long-- what is this here? Not-- not brain, but your head, the shape of your head in a different way, a certain way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said to me, get up. Stand up. And there were 60 girls in this class. They all knew I was Jewish, the only one. They picked me out as a typical Aryan.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did?

speaker: interviewee
text: The teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I said, I'm Jewish. He said, you don't know with whom your mother slept. And he said, what were you ancestors?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were all farmers and cattle dealers. My father's a butcher. He said, you don't know with whom your mother slept. And so of course, the next day I didn't want to go back to that school. My-- my father and mother were still thinking, if I get that German certificate, I could do something with it. Maybe when Hitler is gone, I'll have it in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, '34, '33. Yeah. So I thought that was kind of remarkable. He picked me out from 60 non-Jewish girls, I was a typical 'Aryan.'

speaker: interviewer
text: And I told them they were all Jewish in my background. And I have no idea of any mixed marriages, even. But it's like today, how do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: Way back-- I don't know why I looked like I did. I looked like my father. And-- and you can't go by that. Anyway, that was kind of remarkable for me. It's always stuck in my mind, that he picked me out, let me stand up.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, now that 50 years has passed since the end of the war, is there a message that you would like to leave with us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I kind of mentioned it before, after my experiences. You can't put anybody down. I mean, it's-- for me, that's so logical. We have different cultures. We have different beliefs. We have different-- like, I feel with the other people, it's hard to get together, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: I just read the other day in the papers, they're fighting a religious war. Now, how can we, with our mentality, get together? You know, it's hard. But we have-- there shouldn't be any killings.

speaker: interviewee
text: There shouldn't be ever, a war again. Those young people they grow up now again. They don't know what war is. They have no idea what war is when they want to fight again. But I am pretty pessimistic. I am afraid. Things are going to happen again, especially wars. It's going on right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a totally different view at those things, through my experience. Innocent people suffer. Why? Because of they're born this, they're born that? We don't have any control over it. Besides, I'm satisfied with whatever we are, right? Everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't wish for-- for any-- you shouldn't wish for anything. You're a human being, that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, tape's end.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, tape's rolling. Slowly And Mia, you can see it on the monitor, what I'm getting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And go ahead, Mrs. Haas.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father sent this postcard home to my mother in 1917. He must've been in Romania or somewhere. And he's holding the two little girls. I was thinking he probably thought about his own two little girls back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which one is your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: This is the-- the third from the right, holding the two little girls.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was he doing in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea. No idea. He just said, on the other side it says, regards from your husband and dad.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was he away from home?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the First World War. Oh, that's what you wanted to know. Yeah, he was in the First World War. He was home-- away from home for five years. The war was over in '18. He came home in 1919.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is the name of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Max Buchheim, or Meyer, Meyer.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And that's him?

speaker: interviewer
text: And which is you?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I'm not on that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're not in that? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape's rolling. Five, four, three, two. Any time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, who is this a photo of?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my mother with my sister, who is five years older than I am. So I must be a few months old at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're the baby in the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what-- what is your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilde.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Paula.

speaker: interviewer
text: And their last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Buchheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know the year that this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in 1914. So I suppose it was that year. Because he left for the war right after I was born. He saw me, but he left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin, I suppose. We were never away from there. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: --he didn't see me again till he-- I was five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bring that up. Standby. Tape's rolling. Go ahead and talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Mrs. Haas, who is in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a picture of my mother with my father in uniform. So maybe he came home to visit. I can't remember that. I was just born in '14, when he left. So he might have come home.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you know when this photo was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I suppose 1914.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And can you tell me your father's name, on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Meyer Buchhheim. Oh, he was called Maximilian in Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother is Paula Buchheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who is in this photo, Mrs. Haas?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that's one of the few times my father went with us to a spa in the summertime. On the right-hand side, that's me, about 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: Holding the purse?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Then it's my mother, and comes my father. Uncle and aunt.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your uncle's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Cohen. ?] [INAUDIBLE] [? Cohen, ?] and Hilde. His wife went with her daughter, on the other end, the little one, to South Africa. And she lived there till she was 100 years old. And my uncle died natural death, before that, in Germany. And the girl between my aunt and my cousin is my sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hilde. Hilde.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the time she was Hilde Buchheim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the little girl on the left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Is [? Hannah ?] [? Cohen, ?] the little girl of this couple in the middle.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I suppose in the '20s. I-- I look like I was 11, 12 years old, in 1924 or '25.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a spa where we traveled. My mother always was overweight and went to lose weight. She lost a few pounds and then she came home. [LAUGHS] She put more on than what she lost and went every year. She worked so hard. I don't know how she could have been overweight.

speaker: interviewer
text: What country was it in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was Czechoslovakia, Marienbad. That's where we went a lot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape's rolling. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Haas, where was this picture taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Shanghai. The name of the street was [INAUDIBLE] road. And Henry is on the right-hand side, the boy with his grandmother in the rickshaw. And in front of her is my nephew. Then comes--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your nephew's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Arno ?] [? Shalomar. ?] And in the other rickshaw is my father-in-law, Henry's and these children's grandfather. And in front of him is another relative who lives, today, in Los Angeles.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your father-in-law's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Samuel Haas.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the little boy sitting on his lap? What is his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was [? Heinz ?] [? Shalomar ?] then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And today?

speaker: interviewee
text: Today he's Henry [INAUDIBLE]. His father died of diarrhea in-- in the hospital when he was 30 years old, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
